observations: An upright nonhuman body is enclosed by overlapping pale shell plates arranged into a broad seed- or chrysalis-like oval on two short armored legs.
Dark braided tissue runs between the plates; cracks, grime, and thin blood streaks make the shell look worn and bodily rather than decorative.
A wrapped central organism is exposed through the opening, with a vertical toothed aperture, while a separate pointed structure above carries a narrow purple slit.
The closed frontal symmetry and layered plates emphasize containment and protection more than speed or reach.
defects: No visible arm, weapon, target line, or locomotion cue explains how the upright shell attacks at this moment.
The central wrapped organism is visually subordinate to the repeated pale plates, so the shell reads before the inhabitant.
uncertainties: The image does not resolve whether the upper purple slit or the lower toothed opening is the primary sensing face.
The exact relationship between shell and central organism could be armor, prison, parent body, or separate symbiote.

observations: Three distinct body plans are visible: a very tall closed leaf-blade spire on two legs, a low broad multi-legged shell with an open center, and an upright oval shell exposing a wrapped central figure.
The third silhouette matches the master through its oval layered casing, narrow frontal opening, central occupant, and paired feet.
All candidates retain segmented shell construction while materially changing height, locomotion, opening, and center of mass.
defects: The selected third silhouette has a nearly continuous oval perimeter, so its two legs and central occupant contribute little to the first read.
The black silhouette removes the pale-shell versus dark-tissue separation that carries most of the master's internal hierarchy.
uncertainties: The A, B, and C labels identify alternatives but do not mark which alternative was selected.

observations: The crop shows two separated face-like structures: a pointed upper hood with one vertical purple slit and a lower bound organism whose opening is ringed by several rows of small teeth.
Taut cords cross the lower organism and braided tissue surrounds both structures, making restraint or enforced enclosure directly visible.
The upper slit is closed or pupil-like and the lower mouth remains open, producing a watchful-versus-hungry visual split without human facial muscles.
defects: There is no shared eye-mouth arrangement, so viewer attention divides between two possible faces.
The fixed apertures do not provide brows, gaze direction, or mouth asymmetry from which a precise immediate emotion can be read.
uncertainties: Unaided inspection cannot determine which visible organism owns agency or whether one is controlling the other.

observations: At native size the pale layered oval and two short legs remain immediately visible against the dark background.
The center retains a narrow dark opening, but the bound organism, teeth, upper slit, cords, cracks, and blood streaks collapse into indistinct texture.
The compact symmetry makes the unit read as a single armored pod rather than multiple interacting anatomical parts.
defects: The primary face or aperture cannot be identified at native size.
The repeated shell plates merge into a broad striped oval with little indication of action or facing beyond the feet.
uncertainties: The thumbnail preserves the shell identity but does not resolve whether the dark central mark is a face, opening, or surface seam.
